text:
The2024 Paris Olympicshas kicked off and today marks the beginning of theboxingevents. The sport, which is almost as old as human civilization itself, was part of the ancient Olympic Games, first appearing in 688 BC, and has been a mainstay in the modern Olympics since 1920.

As one of the most popular and widespread sports of the 21st century, boxing is set to once again enthral Olympic audiences, with 13 events and 39 medals on offer in Paris. However, it's worth noting that boxing at the Games differs from professional circuit boxing.

Here,Express Sportdelves into these differences to provide all the necessary information for both boxing enthusiasts and casual viewers.

Olympic boxing matches for both men and women consist of three rounds of three minutes each. There is a one-minute break between each round. The rationale behind such short-round fights is to safeguard the athletes, many of whom will have to compete multiple times throughout the Games.

Before the Rio 2016 Olympics, the scoring system in Olympic boxing mirrored that of amateur boxing, which is solely based on the number of hits landed. However, eight years ago, following a rule change allowing professional boxers to participate in the Games for the first time, the 10-point-must system was introduced.

For those unfamiliar, professional boxing utilises the 10-point-must system, where judges score each round individually on a 10-point scale, with the round's victor receiving 10 points. However, unlike in professional boxing, Olympic boxing employs five judges instead of three.



At the end of every round, these judges determine a winner based on the number of hits landed and award that individual 10 points. The round's loser can receive anywhere between seven and nine points, depending on their performance.

Once the bout concludes, the judges' scorecards are tallied to determine the final winner. This could be unanimous (if all five judges agree that the winner has won two or more rounds) or by split decision. Victories can also be achieved through knockout, referee stoppage, disqualification or abandonment (in the event a fighter gives up or their corner throws the towel in).

If, however, the total scores awarded by each judge - including any deductions - are equal at the end of the bout, the judge(s) with equal scores are asked to nominate which boxer, in their opinion, is the bout's winner in the following cases:

If a boxer receives a warning from the referee, they will lose a point. If they receive three warnings during a single match, they will be automatically disqualified.

Fouls are committed for violations such as holding, improper hitting, wrestling and low blows.

Discovery+is the new home of the Olympics. The streamer is set to broadcast 3,800 hours of sport live from Paris.

Sports fans can getDiscovery+ for £3.99a month until the end of 2024 via Amazon Prime's video channels. This also comes with anAmazon Prime 30-day free trial.

At the Paris Games, all boxers are required to wear gumshields and professional hand wraps (bandages), with head guards now only a requirement for women since their removal from the men's competition in 2016. Additionally, specific glove regulations must be adhered to:

Men



Women

The Paris 2024 Olympics will feature a total of 13 boxing events; seven for men and six for women. These events are divided into various weight categories, as follows:

Men

title: Olympic boxing rules and scoring – How bouts are decided at Paris 2024 | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Harry Brent
description: The boxing events at the Paris 2024 Olympics get underway today, so Express Sport has taken a look at the rules and how the scoring system works
